key_points: Motion is always relative and depends on the chosen frame of reference
Displacement can be positive, negative or zero depending on direction
Uniform circular motion involves constant speed but continuously changing velocity
Acceleration can occur even when speed remains constant if direction changes
Equations of motion apply only when acceleration is uniform
Distance–time graphs help determine speed through slope analysis
Velocity–time graphs help determine both acceleration and displacement
Free falling objects experience constant acceleration due to gravity
Vector quantities like velocity and acceleration require both magnitude and direction
Graphical analysis is a powerful method to study and interpret motion
examples: Example of zero displacement but non-zero distance: Completing one full round on a circular track
Example of uniform circular motion: Motion of Earth around the Sun
Example of acceleration due to direction change: Car turning around a curved road
Example of free fall motion: A stone dropped from a building
Example of constant acceleration: Object falling under gravity
Example of graph interpretation: Triangular area under velocity-time graph giving displacement
exam_tips: Identify the type of motion before selecting formulas
Remember that circular motion always involves acceleration
Use velocity-time graph area to calculate displacement
Check units carefully when converting km/h to m/s
Practice solving numerical problems using equations of motion
